steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.Deus: 'Deus' is a noun meaning 'God' — here in the vocative
hints.peccatori: 'Peccatori' is a noun meaning 'sinner' in the dative

hints.to eat: 'To eat' is 'edere' — 'esto' is the future imperative of 'esse' (to be)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be 'est' or 'es' — 'esto' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sis' — 'esto' is the imperative form
hints.present imperative: Present imperative of 'esse' is 'es' — 'esto' is technically the future imperative, used in prayers

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.sisto, sistere, stiti, statum: These are the parts of 'sistere' (to halt/place)

hints.nominative: 'Deus' is the same form in nominative, but here God is being addressed directly — vocative
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Deum' — 'Deus' is vocative of direct address

hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'me' — 'mihi' is distinctly dative

steps.reveal: peccatori = to a sinner (dat., in apposition with 'mihi')

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'peccator' — 'peccatori' with -i is dative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'peccatoris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'peccatoris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'peccatoris' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What is the relationship between 'mihi' and 'peccatori'?
choices: two separate datives of different function

hints.indirect object and direct object: Both are dative — 'peccatori' is in apposition to 'mihi', not a different case
hints.two separate datives of different functi: Both datives refer to the same person — the speaker who is a sinner
hints.predicate nominative: A predicate nominative would be in the nominative case — 'peccatori' is dative

translation: God, be merciful to me, a sinner
